review_text: Not such a great way to fly. I booked an air ticket and the flight departure timing was brought forward by 7 hours. We had the option to accept, reschedule or cancel to get a refund. I chose the third option but was put thru multiple individuals who do not seem to know what they are doing (each contradicting the other) but eventually, I received an email to say the refund was being processed and it would take 6 weeks. Well, it was the 6th week yesterday and I wrote in to enquire, only to be told they will be processing it now. Today I received another email (a proper refund notice this time) to say it will be another 6 weeks for the refund to come thru.
issue: Flight departure time was moved up by 7 hours, causing inconvenience.
fixes: Deploy an automated email/SMS trigger in the airline’s CRM (e.g., Salesforce Marketing Cloud) that sends a personalized notice with at least 24‑hour lead‑time whenever any flight’s departure time shifts more than 30 minutes from its original schedule. | - Integrate a real‑time “departure change” widget into the mobile app and website using the FlightAware API so passengers see updated times instantly, with a built‑in link to rebook or request a refund via the existing self‑service portal. | - Create a dedicated “Reschedule Assistance” queue in Zendesk with a SLA of 15 minutes; staff receive a Slack alert when a passenger clicks the “Change Request” button, and the ticket is auto‑assigned to the nearest agent with pre‑populated itinerary data. | - Launch a compensation voucher program through the Amadeus Ancillary Services module that automatically issues a $50 travel credit to affected passengers whose departure time changes by more than 60 minutes, with redemption tracked in the loyalty database.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the flight schedule change issue with precise, actionable steps. Each specifies real tools (Salesforce, FlightAware, Zendesk, Amadeus), measurable triggers (30/60 minutes), and clear outcomes. Feasibility is penalized at 3 because integrating FlightAware API and Amadeus Ancillary Services requires non-trivial coordination, third-party licensing, and system integration—moderate effort, not trivial or low-cost.

Well, it was the 6th week yesterday and I wrote in to enquire, only to be told they will be processing it now. Today I received another email (a proper refund notice this time) to say it will be another 6 weeks for the refund to come thru.
issue: Customer service representatives gave contradictory information and seemed incompetent.
fixes: Deploy a mandatory “Knowledge Confidence Quiz” in Lessonly after every product‑update training session; agents must achieve ≥90 % on the quiz before they can handle live tickets. | - Integrate a real‑time “Consistency Alert” into Zendesk that flags any response containing conflicting keywords (e.g., “price” vs. “discount”) and routes it automatically to a senior specialist for review. | - Implement weekly 20‑minute role‑play drills recorded on Loom, with performance scored against a rubric posted on the team’s Power BI dashboard, and tie results to quarterly bonus calculations. | - Launch a searchable, version‑controlled SOP library in Confluence that includes step‑by‑step flowcharts and embeds a “Last Updated” badge; require agents to click a confirmation button in Salesforce Service Cloud before closing any ticket to verify they’ve consulted the latest guidance.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant, actionable, and concrete, naming specific tools (Lessonly, Zendesk, Loom, Power BI, Confluence, Salesforce) and measurable actions. However, feasibility is rated 2 due to high effort: requiring integration of real-time alerts in Zendesk, mandatory role-play recordings with performance scoring tied to bonuses, and a version-controlled SOP library with mandatory confirmation clicks—all of which demand significant cross-team coordination, custom development, and cultural change, making them far from trivial or low-effort to implement.

issue: Refund processing took excessively long, with repeated 6‑week delays.
fixes: Deploy a dedicated refund workflow in Salesforce Service Cloud with a 48‑hour SLA trigger that auto‑escalates any ticket exceeding 24 hours to a senior manager via a Slack integration. | - Integrate Stripe’s Refunds API into your order‑management system (e.g., Shopify) so refunds are processed instantly and logged automatically in your Zendesk ticketing dashboard. | - Schedule weekly 30‑minute “refund sprint” meetings where the finance and operations leads review pending refunds on a Power BI report, assign owners, and commit to resolving at least 80 % of open cases within 7 days. | - Implement automated email notifications through SendGrid that send customers a status update every 72 hours, including a unique case number and expected resolution date, and track open‑ticket response times in real time. | - Launch a self‑service refund portal powered by Intercom’s Help Center that lets customers submit requests, view progress, and receive instant acknowledgment, reducing manual hand‑offs and cutting average processing time from weeks to days.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the 6-week refund delay issue with specific, measurable actions. Each includes concrete tools (Salesforce, Stripe, Zendesk, Power BI, SendGrid, Intercom) and exact metrics (48-hour SLA, 72-hour updates, 80% resolution in 7 days). Actions are clear and executable. Feasibility is capped at 3 due to the need for cross-system integration (e.g., Stripe API into Shopify), new workflows in Salesforce, and launching a self-service portal—moderate effort requiring coordination and development time, not trivial or zero-cost.

review_text: The check-in process at JFK was the worst check-in experience I've experienced. I was waiting for check-in for over 2 hours. The line just didn't move at all. The agent was extremely inefficient. I was one of the last customers to check in and it was about 30 minutes before the departure time. The counter agents was escorting a group of last-to-check-in customers to the security checkpoint so they could skip the line (we were late) and somehow I was the only one who left in the security line on my own. The service on board was terrible despite the high-regarded in-flight service that Singapore Airlines is usually known for. The service was so slow for a flight with only 160 customers.  I sat at one of the single seat toward the end of the aircraft and the steward kept bumping into me like I was sitting on the aisle. He didn't even apologize. My food was OK at best. The worst part was probably the lavatories. Truly disgusting, trash was everywhere on the floor.
issue: Check-in wait time exceeded two hours and the line moved very slowly, with inefficient agents.
fixes: Deploy self‑service check‑in kiosks integrated with the existing reservation database (e.g., Oracle Hospitality OPERA) and set a target of processing ≤ 15 minutes per guest, reducing agent load by at least 20 %. | - Implement a real‑time queue dashboard in Power BI that pulls data from the POS and displays average wait times; trigger automatic alerts to supervisors when wait exceeds 10 minutes so they can deploy additional staff immediately. | - Reassign agents based on a weekly demand‑forecasting model built in Tableau, matching peak‑hour staffing levels to predicted arrival rates and measuring success by keeping the “agents per guest” ratio below 0.7 during high traffic windows. | - Introduce a mobile pre‑check‑in workflow via the airline’s app that captures passport/boarding pass scans, allowing guests to skip the document verification step on arrival and cutting in‑person processing time by up to 40 %.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the core issues: long wait times and inefficient agents. Each specifies exact tools (Oracle OPERA, Power BI, Tableau, airline app), measurable targets (≤15 min, 10-min alert, 0.7 ratio, 40% cut), and clear actions. Feasibility is rated 3 because while tools are named, integrating self-service kiosks and mobile pre-check-in requires significant coordination, new workflows, and potential hardware/software investment—moderate effort, not trivial.

fixes: Deploy a real‑time cabin crew performance dashboard in Power BI that pulls data from seat‑back tablets (e.g., SITA CabinConnect) to flag any service activity exceeding a 2‑minute threshold, enabling supervisors to intervene instantly. | - Introduce a “Service Sprint” schedule using the Kronos workforce management system to assign fixed 15‑minute windows per flight segment where crew complete all meal and beverage deliveries, with compliance tracked via QR‑code check‑ins on crew handhelds. | - Launch a post‑flight mobile survey through Qualtrics that asks passengers to rate “speed of service” on a 1‑5 scale; automatically route scores ≤3 to a dedicated “Service Recovery” Slack channel for immediate follow‑up within 24 hours. | - Conduct weekly 20‑minute VR debriefings on Oculus Quest 2, replaying recorded cabin interactions and assigning corrective micro‑tasks measured by completion rates logged in the existing LMS (Cornerstone). | - Install RFID‑enabled tray‑cart sensors linked to a Tableau inventory board that alerts staff when cart load exceeds a 10‑item limit, ensuring faster restocking during peak service periods.

However, feasibility is severely compromised: VR debriefings on Oculus Quest 2, RFID-enabled tray-cart sensors, and real-time Tableau dashboards tied to SITA CabinConnect require major capital investment, integration work, and hardware deployment—none of which are trivial or low-cost. These exceed even moderate effort, warranting a 2 on feasibility.

review_text: Siem Reap to Kolkata via Singapore. We chose Singapore as we had heard lots of wonderful things about the airline. Our experience was worse than any bucket-shop airlines and we thought Singapore was a world standard airline. On the Siem Reap to Singapore Route my seat had a broken table which I was informed only on the way to the boarding the flight when I was suddenly being pressurised by their ground staff. Although I was waiting at the airport near the gate for over an hour none of your ground staff bothered to discuss this matter with me.  Furthermore the table was only secured with sticky tape which kept falling on my lap and on a couple of occasions fell on my watch! We then received further shoddy service from ground staff at Singapore on the Kolkata flight. Having paid USD 700+ for our one-way ticket from REP to CCU I was shocked to be downgraded to an airline with filthy seats, no TV, no leg space and in general badly in need of an uplift. To cap it all the cabin staff offered me gifts worth SGD 75 on the plane. This offer got progressively downgraded to a few miles on their airline.
issue: Seat had a broken table that was only disclosed at boarding and was poorly secured with sticky tape, causing it to fall on the passenger’s lap and watch
fixes: Deploy a digital seat‑inspection checklist in iAuditor (SafetyCulture) that crew complete before boarding; require photo evidence of any defect and automatic escalation to maintenance if “broken table” is flagged. | - Install RFID tags on all seat tables and integrate them with the aircraft’s diagnostic system so that any loose or missing tag triggers an immediate alert on the cabin crew’s handheld device (e.g., iPad with ServiceNow Mobile). | - Equip each galley with a pre‑packed Seat‑Repair Kit containing quick‑fix adhesive tape, hardware kits, and a standardized SOP sheet hosted in the airline’s LMS (e.g., Cornerstone), ensuring crew have the right tools within reach. | - Configure the aircraft’s onboard entertainment system to display a short video highlighting proper seat‑assembly steps during boarding; track view counts via the existing analytics dashboard and mandate ≥90 % completion rates per flight. | - Implement a post‑flight passenger survey question (“Was your seat table secure?”) linked to a real‑time Power BI dashboard that flags any negative response for immediate follow‑up by the cabin operations team.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant to the specific issue of defective seat tables, clearly actionable with precise steps, and grounded in real tools (iAuditor, RFID, ServiceNow, LMS, Power BI). However, feasibility is rated 2 due to high effort: RFID integration with aircraft diagnostics, real-time alerts on handhelds, and onboard video deployment require major hardware, software, and operational changes—impossible to implement today without months of development, testing, and cost. This violates the strict feasibility standard for a 'trivial' or 'low-effort' solution.

fixes: Deploy IoT‑enabled cleaning sensors (e.g., VergeSense) on all cabin zones to trigger automatic work orders in the existing FMX maintenance platform when surface contamination exceeds preset thresholds, ensuring real‑time cleanliness verification. | - Integrate a cloud‑based media library with seat‑back screens via Viasat’s Ka‑band satellite link and schedule quarterly content refreshes tracked in Asana, guaranteeing at least one new movie per month across all routes. | - Install modular, height‑adjustable seats sourced from Recaro Aerospace and program the airline’s crew management system (e.g., Sabre AirCentre) to enforce a minimum legroom policy measured by a calibrated leg‑space gauge during pre‑flight checks. | - Launch a post‑flight micro‑survey through Qualtrics embedded in the mobile app, targeting a 90 % completion rate within 24 hours; any rating ≤3 triggers an immediate “clean‑up” task in the same FMX workflow. | - Establish a “Clean‑Air” certification program using HEPA‑rated air filters from Honeywell and log filter replacement cycles in the aircraft’s CMMS (e.g., AMOS), aiming for a <0.01 µg/m³ particulate count measured by onboard particle counters.
rationale: Relevance is 5: Each recommendation directly targets a specific aspect of cabin comfort (cleanliness, entertainment, legroom, feedback, air quality). Actionability is 4: Steps are clear but assume access to systems like FMX and Sabre AirCentre without confirming they’re already integrated or accessible. Feasibility is 2: Installing IoT sensors, modular seats, and satellite-linked media systems requires major capital investment, hardware changes, and integration across multiple vendors—far beyond trivial or low-effort. This is high effort, expensive, and not deployable 'today' with existing tools.

review_text: We were given a flight with Scoot from Bangkok to Singapore, and then with Singapore to Auckland. The flight with Scoot was perfectly acceptable but what was not was that they decided that we must collect our boarding pass in Singapore which might have been OK but they they never told us where to collect them from, so we we spent ages trying to find the location and only just made it to the gate in time. The previous leg from Milan to Hanoi involved a Scoot flight and we received all our passes in Milan. On the flight when meals were served the crew just appeared at the seat with a tray whilst we were dozing and expected us to put out the table. As we had ordered book the cook, the main meal service took a while and we had finished our meal before drinks were offered. Premium economy food was no better than Economy fare and considering the cost of the flight a little more thought could have gone into the meal.
issue: Passengers were not informed where to collect their boarding passes in Singapore, causing confusion and a rushed arrival at the gate.
fixes: Deploy QR‑code signage at the arrivals hall linked to a live “Boarding Pass Collection” map on the airline’s mobile app (using Google Maps API) that updates automatically when new gates are assigned. | - Integrate real‑time push notifications into the airline’s app and SMS channel via Twilio, triggering a “Gate X – Collect Boarding Pass Now” alert 15 minutes before departure for all passengers with confirmed seats. | - Install digital kiosks at immigration and baggage claim that display a static image of the current boarding pass collection desk location and its floor number, refreshed every minute from the central itinerary feed. | - Create a pre‑arrival email/SMS template in HubSpot that includes a clickable link to a one‑page PDF showing the exact desk number and gate assignment, sent 24 hours and again 90 minutes before departure.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the core issue: lack of clear, timely boarding pass collection information in Singapore. Each is specific, uses real tools (Twilio, Google Maps API, HubSpot, central itinerary feed), and includes measurable actions. Feasibility is 3 because while tools are real, integrating live updates across multiple systems (app, SMS, kiosks, email) requires cross-team coordination, API maintenance, and potential hardware deployment—moderate effort, not trivial.

review_text: We were given a flight with Scoot from Bangkok to Singapore, and then with Singapore to Auckland. The flight with Scoot was perfectly acceptable but what was not was that they decided that we must collect our boarding pass in Singapore which might have been OK but they they never told us where to collect them from, so we we spent ages trying to find the location and only just made it to the gate in time. The previous leg from Milan to Hanoi involved a Scoot flight and we received all our passes in Milan. On the flight when meals were served the crew just appeared at the seat with a tray whilst we were dozing and expected us to put out the table. As we had ordered book the cook, the main meal service took a while and we had finished our meal before drinks were offered. Premium economy food was no better than Economy fare and considering the cost of the flight a little more thought could have gone into the meal.
issue: In-flight crew served meals while passengers were dozing and expected tables to be set, leading to poor service timing and inconvenience.
fixes: Deploy a cabin‑crew schedule optimizer (e.g., CrewConnect) that automatically assigns meal‑service windows based on real‑time passenger activity data from seat‑back sensors, ensuring meals are served when the majority are awake and tables are cleared before dozing resumes. | - Install RFID‑enabled table‑setting kits with built‑in timers that trigger a “set‑table” alert in the crew’s handheld device (e.g., SITA Cabin Service app) at the pre‑defined service interval, eliminating manual coordination errors. | - Integrate a digital service checklist into the existing iPad‑based service workflow (using the airline’s existing Amadeus SkySuite module) that requires crew to log a “meal‑served” and “table‑cleared” entry, with compliance tracked and reported daily to operations managers via Power BI dashboards. | - Launch a post‑flight micro‑survey through the airline’s mobile app that asks passengers to rate “timing of meal service” and “promptness of table clearing”; set a target ≥90 % positive score and review results weekly to adjust scheduling parameters in the optimizer.
rationale: Relevance is 5: all recommendations directly address meal timing and table clearance issues. Actionability is 4: steps are clear but assume access to unverified systems like seat-back sensors and RFID kits. Concreteness is 5: specific tools (SITA, Amadeus SkySuite, Power BI) and metrics (90% target) are named. Feasibility is 2: deploying seat-back sensors and RFID table kits requires massive hardware investment, integration, and retrofitting—high effort, not trivial or low-cost, violating the strict feasibility standard.

issue: The quality of premium economy meals was no better than economy fare, disappointing given the higher cost.
fixes: Deploy a quarterly third‑party sensory audit (e.g., through the FoodLogiQ platform) with a minimum score threshold (e.g., ≥8/10) that automatically triggers a supplier performance review if scores fall below target. | - Integrate QR‑code meal feedback into the airline’s mobile app (using Qualtrics Experience Management) and set a KPI that ≥85 % of premium economy passengers rate “meal quality” as “good” or better within two weeks of service. | - Launch a dedicated premium‑economy catering line by partnering with at least three regional chefs; track ingredient costs in SAP Ariba and enforce a minimum 15 % cost differential over standard economy fare for each menu item. | - Implement a real‑time inventory tracking system (such as IBM Maximo) to ensure only premium‑grade proteins, seafood, and produce are used on premium flights, with daily variance alerts sent to the catering manager when stock falls below a 95 % premium‑quality threshold.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant to food quality in premium economy, explicitly name real tools (e.g., FoodLogiQ, Qualtrics, SAP Ariba, IBM Maximo), and are actionable with clear steps. However, feasibility is severely compromised: implementing a third-party sensory audit, integrating QR feedback via Qualtrics, launching a dedicated catering line with regional chefs, and deploying IBM Maximo for real-time tracking require significant budget, cross-functional coordination, and new vendor contracts—none of which are trivial or low-effort. These are high-effort, high-cost changes, warranting a 2 on feasibility.

review_text: The ground crew were unreasonable and very rude. We were denied boarding as according to them we were 4 minutes late.  The ground crew did a lousy job of offloading our luggage. We had to wait almost half a day in the baggage carousel before filling a missing bag report.
issue: Ground crew were rude and denied boarding for a minor 4‑minute delay
fixes: Deploy a tablet‑based “Passenger Service” app (e.g., using ServiceNow Mobile) on all ground‑crew handhelds that logs real‑time delay reasons and automatically displays approved boarding waivers for delays < 10 minutes, eliminating manual denials. | - Implement a weekly 15‑minute microlearning module on empathy and de‑escalation in the LMS (Cornerstone), with a mandatory 90 % quiz score required before crew members are scheduled for duty. | - Install a QR‑code feedback kiosk at every gate that feeds instant satisfaction scores into Power BI dashboards; set a daily alert threshold of ≤ 80 % positive ratings and trigger a corrective‑action ticket in Jira for any breach. | - Create a “Delay Exception SOP” checklist in Confluence that outlines authorized grounds for waiver (minor delay, medical emergency, etc.) and require supervisors to sign off electronically via DocuSign before crew can deny boarding.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant to service quality and the specific issue of rude ground crew denying boarding. Each is actionable with clear steps and concrete tools (ServiceNow Mobile, Cornerstone, Power BI, Jira, Confluence, DocuSign). However, feasibility is low: deploying a new tablet-based app across all ground crews, integrating it with existing systems, and requiring electronic sign-offs via DocuSign for every boarding denial is high-effort, costly, and not trivial to implement. The solution assumes existing infrastructure and approval workflows that may not exist, making it unrealistic for immediate rollout.

issue: Luggage was mishandled, leading to a half‑day wait and a missing bag report
fixes: Deploy RFID‑enabled baggage tags and integrate them with the existing SITA BagManager system; configure the software to automatically generate a “missing bag” alert within 10 minutes of tag loss detection. | - Implement a real‑time passenger service dashboard in Power BI that pulls data from the airline’s OMS (e.g., Amadeus) and flags any luggage delay >15 min, triggering an immediate SMS/WhatsApp notification to affected travelers. | - Introduce a dedicated “Mishandling Response Team” staffed by two agents per shift who follow a scripted SOP stored in Confluence, with a KPI that 95 % of missed‑bag reports are resolved within 30 minutes of submission. | - Conduct weekly root‑cause analysis reviews using a standardized RCA template in Miro, assigning corrective actions and tracking completion rates via a Trello board with due dates and owner assignments.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant, actionable, and concrete, naming specific tools (SITA BagManager, Power BI, Amadeus, Confluence, Miro, Trello). These are not trivial or low-effort changes.

review_text: Our first flight was delayed more than one hour, so we lost our connecting flight. The new booking provided was horrible. I was travelling with my four-year-old and the new seat provided is far away, and we were separated. All the family members are separated for the whole journey. Miscommunication between the crew members made us to walk between the terminals for 2 times. Didn't get any help from the crew, even though we requested to provide a seat together.
issue: Flight was delayed over an hour, causing a missed connecting flight
fixes: Deploy a real‑time disruption management platform (e.g., Amadeus Altéa DDM) that automatically rebooks affected passengers onto alternative flights within 15 minutes of a delay detection, with a KPI target of ≥ 95 % on‑time rebooking. | - Integrate FlightAware’s predictive delay alerts into the crew scheduling software so that ground staff receive a 10‑minute advance warning of any forecasted delay exceeding 20 minutes, enabling proactive gate hold or standby crew assignments. | - Implement Twilio SMS/WhatsApp notifications linked to the airline’s mobile app that push personalized delay updates, rebooking options, and alternate itinerary suggestions to affected travelers instantly. | - Use IBM Watson Assistant to create a self‑service chatbot on the website and app that lets passengers view current status, request assistance, and confirm new itineraries without human intervention, aiming for a resolution rate of ≥ 90 % within 5 minutes of query submission.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant to flight delay remediation, explicitly actionable with clear steps, and concrete with specific tools and KPIs. However, feasibility is rated 2 due to the high cost and complexity of deploying Amadeus Altéa DDM, integrating FlightAware with crew scheduling, and implementing IBM Watson Assistant at scale—none of which are trivial or low-effort. These require significant infrastructure investment, cross-system integration, and likely months of development, making them infeasible for a budget-conscious COO.

review_text: I embarked on a journey with high hopes and anticipation, having secured a connecting flight ticket from Penang to Singapore, and then onwards to Jakarta. Opting for Singapore Airlines, renowned for its outstanding reputation in both air travel and service, I anticipated nothing less than a seamless experience. The cost of this connecting flight amounted to MYR 1601, roughly USD 335, a price I was more than willing to pay for quality service. My journey commenced with a minor delay in takeoff from Penang. The flight proceeded smoothly, accompanied by a light snack comprising a slice of pizza and a beverage of choice. As we approached Changi Airport, the situation took an unexpected turn. The captain announced a delay in landing, estimating an additional 15 to 20 minutes before touchdown. A sense of unease crept over me as my connecting flight departure time loomed closer. Despite my concerns, I remained optimistic, trusting in the efficiency of Singapore Airlines to navigate any challenges seamlessly. Upon landing at 12:05 pm and disembarking at 12:18 pm, I was met with a startling realization. Rather than a smooth transition to my connecting flight, I was handed a new ticket, rescheduled for departure at 4:10 pm, accompanied by a SGD 15 food voucher for Burger King and Coffee Bean.  I sought clarity from the attendant, only to be met with dismissiveness. The absence of any acknowledgment or attempt at redress left me deeply disappointed. The disregard for my time and the indifferent handling of the situation underscored a gap in the airline's commitment to customer service. In retrospect, my expectation for exemplary hospitality and proactive problem-solving from a reputed airline like Singapore Airlines was regrettably unmet. As I reflect on this experience, I am reminded of the importance of genuine care and consideration in customer service, particularly in moments of inconvenience or distress.
issue: The landing delay caused a missed connecting flight and forced a rebooking to a much later departure, disrupting the travel itinerary.
fixes: Deploy an automated “Missed‑Connection Recovery” workflow in the airline’s CRM (e.g., Salesforce Service Cloud) that instantly offers affected passengers a pre‑approved alternative flight on the next available departure and triggers a compensation voucher within 15 minutes of the delay detection. | - Integrate FlightAware’s real‑time delay feed into the crew dispatch console so ground staff receive instant alerts when a landing is delayed by more than 10 minutes, allowing them to proactively rebook eligible passengers onto later flights before they miss their connections. | - Implement a dedicated “Connectivity Assurance” team with a KPI of resolving 95 % of missed‑connection incidents within 20 minutes, tracked via a Power BI dashboard that pulls data from the airline’s reservation system (Sabre) and logs every resolution time stamp. | - Launch a self‑service mobile app feature that automatically sends push notifications and displays alternative itineraries sourced from Amadeus API, giving passengers the option to rebook themselves while still at the gate and reducing manual rebooking queues.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the root cause of missed connections due to landing delays. Each is highly actionable with clear steps and specific tools (Salesforce, FlightAware, Sabre, Amadeus, Power BI). Concreteness is strong with named systems and metrics. Feasibility is 3 because implementing a new CRM workflow, integrating FlightAware, and launching a self-service app feature require cross-team coordination, API integration, and development effort—moderate to high cost and time, not trivial or zero-cost.

issue: The compensation offered was limited to a small food voucher, which was insufficient for the inconvenience caused by the missed connection.
fixes: Deploy a rule‑based compensation engine in Salesforce Service Cloud that automatically escalates any “missed‑connection” case to a $150 travel‑expense reimbursement plus a complimentary lounge access voucher when the original voucher amount falls below the predefined threshold (e.g., ≤ $50). | - Integrate a real‑time passenger disruption dashboard with Amadeus Altéa API to trigger an instant offer of a higher‑value meal voucher (e.g., $75) and a pre‑booked hotel room via the airline’s partner booking platform (e.g., Hotelogix) within 15 minutes of the incident. | - Implement a post‑incident NPS survey sent through Qualtrics that includes a mandatory open‑ended question on perceived compensation adequacy; route any response rating ≤ 6 to a dedicated Compensation Review Team for immediate follow‑up and adjustment of the compensation matrix. | - Establish a weekly KPI report in Power BI tracking “Compensation Adequacy Rate” (percentage of missed‑connection cases where the offered compensation met or exceeded the predefined threshold) and tie the result to quarterly performance bonuses for operations and revenue management teams.

review_text: The flight was booked at a time when the Covid situation was still looking better and the chance to be be able to leave Changi airport during a long layover looked good. That face with Singapore Airlines flexibility claim of change of flights on their website, made me booking a flight with a 16h layover. Unfortunately, things were not looking good about a month ago, and I tried to change my flights to a short layover. I tried to contact SQ via its change of flight form for Covid related reasons through their website. 5 requests over the span of a month remain unanswered till today, complaint emails were also not replied to. I also tried to contact SQ through their callcenter, was placed in a loop for hours. After getting through after days of trying, I was told that there were seats available for the same booking class and I would be informed once the the ticket would be re-issued. So far so good. An hour later I was called, saying that this „special fare ticket“ can only be changed if if I pay this and that in addition, which ended up to a ridiculous amount, and I declined. So I was back to square and had to endure a 16h stay at Changi airport. SQ does not honor their claim of ticket flexibility. Other carrier I flew during the pandemic were willing honor their flexibility claims. The flights themselves were rather unremarkable, not as polished as Qatar, with no dining on demand offered. Food was average to say the least. No touchscreen TV and average seat on the really noisy 777ER on the long flight. Overall due to the non existing customer service on the ground, not recommended
issue: Customer service was unresponsive; multiple contact attempts via website, email, and call center received no reply or long hold times.
fixes: Deploy a ticketing platform such as Zendesk with SLA rules (e.g., 24‑hour first response) and integrate it tightly into your CRM so every inbound email, chat, and phone inquiry auto‑creates a ticket and triggers real‑time alerts to the on‑call team. | - Implement a cloud‑based call‑center solution like Five9 with predictive routing and automatic callback features, then monitor average answer time daily against a target of ≤ 45 seconds. | - Set up automated acknowledgment emails via SendGrid that include a unique case ID and expected resolution window, and track open rates and follow‑up actions in your analytics dashboard. | - Schedule weekly “live‑chat shadow” drills where agents handle mock chats from a pre‑recorded script library hosted on Lessonly, recording completion metrics and correcting any missed steps before they go live.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the customer's core issues: unresponsiveness and long hold times. Each specifies concrete tools (Zendesk, Five9, SendGrid, Lessonly), measurable actions (SLA rules, target answer time, open rate tracking), and clear execution steps. Feasibility is reduced to 3 due to the need for integrating multiple new systems (CRM, Five9, SendGrid, Lessonly) and training, which exceeds low-effort thresholds despite being possible with moderate effort.

review_text: The flight was booked at a time when the Covid situation was still looking better and the chance to be be able to leave Changi airport during a long layover looked good. That face with Singapore Airlines flexibility claim of change of flights on their website, made me booking a flight with a 16h layover. Unfortunately, things were not looking good about a month ago, and I tried to change my flights to a short layover. I tried to contact SQ via its change of flight form for Covid related reasons through their website. 5 requests over the span of a month remain unanswered till today, complaint emails were also not replied to. I also tried to contact SQ through their callcenter, was placed in a loop for hours. After getting through after days of trying, I was told that there were seats available for the same booking class and I would be informed once the the ticket would be re-issued. So far so good. An hour later I was called, saying that this „special fare ticket“ can only be changed if if I pay this and that in addition, which ended up to a ridiculous amount, and I declined. So I was back to square and had to endure a 16h stay at Changi airport. SQ does not honor their claim of ticket flexibility. Other carrier I flew during the pandemic were willing honor their flexibility claims. The flights themselves were rather unremarkable, not as polished as Qatar, with no dining on demand offered. Food was average to say the least. No touchscreen TV and average seat on the really noisy 777ER on the long flight. Overall due to the non existing customer service on the ground, not recommended
issue: Lack of dining on demand and average-quality food during the flight.
fixes: Deploy a cloud‑based catering management platform (e.g., MealPro) integrated with the airline’s inventory system to allow passengers to pre‑order meals via the mobile app or seat‑back screen, ensuring real‑time availability and reducing last‑minute gaps. | - Introduce a digital “Dining Dashboard” in the crew’s tablet app that tracks meal preparation time, temperature, and passenger satisfaction scores in real time; set automated alerts when prep exceeds 15 minutes so corrective actions can be taken immediately. | - Implement a quarterly supplier audit using the ISO 22000 certification checklist and require a minimum 95 % on‑time delivery score before any contract renewal, guaranteeing consistent food quality and freshness. | - Launch a post‑flight micro‑survey linked to the CRM (e.g., Salesforce Service Cloud) that asks passengers to rate food quality on a 1‑5 scale; feed results into a Power BI report with weekly KPI dashboards and trigger a root‑cause analysis if average ratings fall below 3.5.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant, actionable, and concrete, naming specific tools (MealPro, Salesforce, Power BI, ISO 22000) and measurable actions. However, feasibility is scored 2 because deploying a cloud-based catering platform (MealPro), integrating it with inventory systems, and rolling out new dashboards and surveys across fleets requires significant budget, cross-team coordination, and system integration—far beyond trivial or low effort. This is high-effort, expensive, and not immediately feasible without major investment.

rationale: Relevance is 5: all recommendations directly address the lack of touchscreen IFE. Actionability is 4: steps are clear but assume access to proprietary systems (e.g., Panasonic Avionics eX3, Netflix Business API) without confirming availability. Concreteness is 5: specific tools (Medallia Mobile SDK, AWS Elemental MediaTailor) and interfaces (HDMI/USB) are named. issue: Seat was average and aircraft was noisy, leading to uncomfortable travel experience.
fixes: Deploy noise‑cancelling cabin speakers (e.g., Bose Aviation Series) and schedule quarterly acoustic audits with calibrated SPL meters to keep cabin noise below 65 dB(A). | - Install seat‑back recline sensors linked to the airline’s maintenance management system (e.g., AMOS) that trigger automatic replacement of any seat exceeding wear thresholds after 12 months of service. | - Introduce a “Comfort Kit” program using RFID‑tagged pillow inserts and lumbar cushions, tracked via the existing inventory module (e.g., SAP MM), with a KPI of ≥90 % passenger satisfaction scores on post‑flight surveys targeting seat comfort. | - Implement a real‑time vibration monitoring dashboard in the flight operations center that alerts technicians when seat frame resonance exceeds 0.5 g, prompting immediate corrective actions before the next departure.

review_text: Check-in was fast, because I could use the business-class check-in counters as a PPS-member. Otherwise endless queues. Boarding was slow because the flight was full and I boarded late with the crowds. No recognition as a Solitaire-PPS-member on board as before. Cabin crew busy with 'sorting' the passengers and their cabin-luggage to not miss our slot. Seat in a 3-3-3 configuration very narrow, seat-pitch ok (for me being 1.7m only) sufficient, however. Left channel of my speakers was not working, even after exchanging them. Internet not working on my phone, either. No help given to connect, as on earlier flights, instead 'proof' was given to me by a flight attendant that Internet was working on her iphone. Not really helpful. No reading materials. Very basic and little food-options offered in a tiny cardboard-box, 'designed for a reason' (whatever this reason may be). Very inattentive inflight-cabin-crew: As for the after dinner drink I was ignored by the them while my two seat-members got served their drinks of choice. Minimum seat-recline, similar to those in budget-airlines. No soap in washroom. No disinfectant-kits offered as advertised, I got one after request only. Wearing a mask, which is 'mandatory' was not re-enforced, a man in the middle seat was wearing his mask under his chin - no one of the crew cared. Overall the crew seemed to be overworked and de-motivated. The typical glass of champagne offered to me as a PPS-member when I raised my concerns did not really help, it just would have been good enough if the cabin-crew would have fulfilled their duties. Disembarking was fast and swift as well as delivery of my checked-in baggage.
issue: Boarding was slow due to a full flight and crew having to sort passengers and luggage
fixes: Deploy RFID‑enabled boarding passes integrated with the airline’s mobile app so that passengers receive real‑time gate‑change notifications and are automatically directed to assigned boarding groups via QR codes at the gate. | - Implement a data‑driven “boarding‑queue” algorithm in the airport’s operations center (e.g., using Tableau dashboards) that prioritizes families, elite status members, and unaccompanied minors based on pre‑flight booking data and pushes updated priority lists to gate staff every 2 minutes. | - Introduce a dedicated “luggage‑sort crew” equipped with handheld barcode scanners linked to the SITA Baggage Management System; the system flags any bags exceeding the standard weight limit before loading and routes them to a separate conveyor belt for immediate offloading. | - Schedule additional cabin crew during peak flights by analyzing historical load factors in Power BI and setting a threshold of >85 % occupancy, then trigger automatic staffing adjustments through the crew rostering module of the Amadeus Altéa platform.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant to boarding delays and luggage sorting. Each is actionable with clear steps and concrete tools (e.g., Tableau, SITA, Amadeus Altéa). However, feasibility is low: deploying RFID-enabled passes, new dedicated crews, and system-wide integrations across multiple platforms (SITA, Amadeus, Power BI) require significant capital, cross-system coordination, and months of rollout—far beyond trivial or low effort. This exceeds even moderate effort (score 3), warranting a 2 on feasibility.

Very inattentive inflight-cabin-crew: As for the after dinner drink I was ignored by the them while my two seat-members got served their drinks of choice. Minimum seat-recline, similar to those in budget-airlines. No soap in washroom. No disinfectant-kits offered as advertised, I got one after request only. Wearing a mask, which is 'mandatory' was not re-enforced, a man in the middle seat was wearing his mask under his chin - no one of the crew cared. Overall the crew seemed to be overworked and de-motivated. The typical glass of champagne offered to me as a PPS-member when I raised my concerns did not really help, it just would have been good enough if the cabin-crew would have fulfilled their duties. Disembarking was fast and swift as well as delivery of my checked-in baggage.
issue: Cabin crew ignored the passenger’s after‑dinner drink request, showing inattentiveness
fixes: Deploy a cabin‑service mobile app (e.g., CrewConnect) with a mandatory “Drink Request Acknowledged” button that logs timestamps and automatically escalates any unattended requests to the galley supervisor within 2 minutes. | - Integrate a real‑time passenger feedback widget into the airline’s mobile app (using Qualtrics Experience Management) that triggers an instant alert to the flight attendant’s tablet when a passenger rates service quality ≤3 stars. | - Conduct a weekly 10‑minute micro‑learning video review on the LMS (e.g., SAP SuccessFactors) focused on after‑meal drink protocols, requiring 100% attendance tracked via completion badges. | - Install RFID‑enabled beverage cart sensors linked to the aircraft’s Wi‑Fi network; each drink placement generates a confirmation email to the assigned seat zone, providing auditable proof of delivery. | - Implement a Power BI KPI dashboard that measures “Average Time to Drink Service” per flight segment, sets a target of <5 min, and flags any deviation for corrective coaching.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant to the specific issue of ignored drink requests, with clear, concrete tools and measurable actions. Each is actionable with precise steps. However, feasibility is severely compromised: RFID sensors, real-time feedback integration, and mandatory app deployment require significant capital investment, hardware installation, and cross-system integration—far beyond trivial or low-effort implementation. These are not 'today' fixes but multi-month, high-cost projects, warranting a 2 on feasibility.

issue: Disinfectant kits were not offered as advertised unless specifically requested
fixes: Deploy QR‑code stickers on all checkout lanes that link to a pre‑filled “Add disinfectant kit” button in the POS (e.g., Square) so customers automatically receive the item at checkout without needing to request it. | - Configure the inventory module in your ERP (e.g., NetSuite) to flag any disinfectant kit order line items with zero quantity and trigger an automatic reorder alert when stock falls below a 7‑day safety threshold. | - Set up a Power BI dashboard that pulls real‑time sales data from the POS and flags any transaction where the disinfectant kit was sold only after explicit request, prompting weekly review meetings to close gaps. | - Create a mandatory checklist in the staff mobile app (e.g., Deputy) that requires associates to confirm “Offered disinfectant kit?” before closing each register; compliance is logged and reviewed daily for ≥95% adherence.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the root issue: disinfectant kits not being offered proactively. Feasibility is rated 3 because while tools exist, integrating QR codes with POS, setting up real-time dashboards, and enforcing mobile app checklists across staff requires moderate coordination, training, and system configuration—beyond trivial rollout.

fixes: Deploy QR‑code “Mask Compliance” checklists integrated into the airline’s mobile app (e.g., via the existing Amadeus Mobile Check‑in API) that crew scan before boarding; non‑compliant entries trigger an automatic alert to the cabin manager within 5 seconds. | - Introduce a real‑time mask‑enforcement dashboard in the crew’s tablet SOP system (using AirWatch or Microsoft Teams Planner) that logs each passenger’s mask status per row and flags any deviation for immediate corrective action. | - Conduct weekly 15‑minute VR scenario drills on the Oculus Quest 2, where crews practice enforcing mask protocols while receiving live feedback from a remote supervisor using the Strivr LMS analytics module. | - Launch a passenger‑feedback kiosk at the gate that sends an SMS survey asking “Did you see the crew enforce mask wearing?” with a built‑in compliance score that is reviewed by operations every shift.

fixes: Deploy a workforce‑management platform (e.g., Kronos WFM) with predictive scheduling that caps weekly overtime to ≤8 hours per employee while ensuring shift coverage meets a 95 % fill rate. | - Introduce a “Shift‑Swap” mobile app (such as WhenIWork) allowing staff to request or approve schedule changes within 24 hours, reducing last‑minute adjustments that lead to overwork. | - Launch a weekly 15‑minute “Mood Pulse” survey via SurveyMonkey linked to a real‑time dashboard in Microsoft Teams; trigger a corrective action plan when average morale drops below 70 %. | - Implement a gamified incentive program using Bonusly that awards points for on‑time arrival, quality audits, and peer recognition, with monthly top‑performer rewards visible on the company intranet.
